items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to Berlin' (by train) in German?
items.why: Students use gehen for any 'going'. In German, gehen = go ON FOOT only. FAHREN = go by vehicle (car, train, bus). FLIEGEN = go by plane. To Berlin by train: ich fahre. Saying 'ich gehe nach Berlin' suggests walking there!
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm travelling to Germany' in German?
items.why: Students use 'in' or 'zu'. For most countries and cities: NACH (Deutschland, Berlin, Paris). For countries with articles (die Schweiz, die USA): IN with accusative. Easy to mix up - nach is for proper place names without articles.

items.q: How do you say 'by plane' in German?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: mit dem Flugzeug. “mit das Flugzeug” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Youth hostel. “Hotel” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many accommodation options are listed in the 'Stay' walkthrough?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
